serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of honey drumlets is 5 pieces, weighing approximately 150 g, commonly served as a starter or side dish at casual dining settings in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Drumlets — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Honey Drumlets (cr_id G00010458). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value from International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; analogous estimate applied to honey-dominant glaze
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB record for Honey Drumlets shows 82.12 g sugar/100g with negligible protein and fat, indicating the nutritional profile is dominated by the honey glaze/marinade rather than the whole cooked chicken dish. GI of 87 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for honey (mean GI ~87, high category), which is the dominant carbohydrate source. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (87 × 82.4 × 1.5) / 100 = 107.9 g. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific HPB dish exists in the University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (82.1 g/100g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.2 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, raising blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this dish in its current form; the honey glaze delivers a very high sugar load that drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and adiposity — both established BP risk factors per AHA 2018 guidelines — and the dish should be reformulated with a low-sugar marinade if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 82.1 g/100g (GI ~87) with only 0.2 g/100g dietary fibre causes rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes must avoid this dish; the honey glaze provides over 80 g of rapidly absorbed simple sugars per 100 g with a high GI of approximately 87, producing extreme postprandial glucose spikes that are incompatible with glycaemic management targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high free sugar intake (82.1 g/100g) is associated with elevated triglycerides, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and increased cardiovascular risk independent of saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; the extremely high free sugar content (82.1 g/100g) is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and VLDL production, increasing cardiovascular risk per WHO 2015 free sugar guidelines, and the dish provides no cardioprotective nutrients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 82.1 g/100g far exceeds the RA refined sugar threshold; high sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-6, TNF-alpha) that worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the extreme honey-derived sugar load (82.1 g/100g) promotes systemic inflammation via upregulation of IL-6 and TNF-alpha, directly worsening RA disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Drumlets is a popular Singaporean-style dish consisting of chicken drumlets (the meaty first segment of the chicken wing) marinated and glazed with honey, typically combined with soy sauce, garlic, and ginger, then baked or grilled until caramelised. The dish is characterised by a sticky, sweet, amber-coloured glaze with a tender, juicy interior. It is commonly served as a starter, snack, or side dish at casual dining restaurants, hawker-style eateries, and home kitchens across Singapore. The HPB nutritional data for this record (cr_id G00010458) reflects a profile dominated by the honey glaze component, with 82.4 g carbohydrate and 82.1 g sugar per 100 g, very low protein (0.3 g), and negligible fat (0.0 g), suggesting the data may represent the marinade/glaze rather than the whole cooked dish. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its extremely high free sugar content and high glycaemic index (~87). A 150 g serving delivers an estimated glycaemic load of approximately 108, which is exceptionally high. The dish provides small amounts of potassium (52 mg/100g), iron (0.42 mg/100g), and calcium (6 mg/100g) but no meaningful micronutrient benefit relative to its sugar burden.

What is the sugar content of honey drumlets?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Honey Drumlets (cr_id G00010458) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database honey entry (GI ~87) as an analogous estimate, which is appropriate given the sugar-dominant nutritional profile.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: fat_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g per the HPB source. This is noted as confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry. The nutritional profile (82.4 g carbohydrate, 0.3 g protein, 0.0 g fat) strongly suggests the HPB data reflects the honey glaze/marinade component rather than the whole cooked chicken dish; the actual whole-dish fat content would be higher. Users should be aware of this data limitation. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 per HPB — confirmed zero per source, consistent with a glaze-dominant profile. Saturated_fat_g is 0.0 per HPB — confirmed zero per source. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish. Detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, and moisture data are not available in the HPB record for G00010458 as of 2026-04-16. 3. FOOD GROUP CORRECTION: The input record classifies this dish under 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients', which is incorrect for a chicken dish. Reclassified to 'Meat and alternatives / Poultry dishes' based on the food's actual composition. 4. GI NOTE: GI of 87 is an analogous estimate based on honey (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this specific HPB dish exists. The extremely high sugar content (82.1 g/100g) makes the high GI classification robust regardless of the exact value. 5. DATA ANOMALY: The per-100g nutritional profile (82.4 g carbohydrate, 0.3 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 304 kcal) is atypical for a whole chicken dish and is consistent with the honey glaze/marinade dominating the HPB measurement. This limitation is preserved from the source without modification per Veritas source integrity rules.
